response_text: Friedberg's core claim: a coalition of Warren/Sanders/Khanna types are arranging power — deciding capital flows, enriching their families — while framing it as justice.

response_text: Chamath draws on his own childhood — family on ~$17-19k/year Canadian welfare — to make the point personal: his father could have taken an $8-10/hr job but didn't, cycling between drinking and not working.

cited_text: He doesn't have one more dollar in the bank than he did the day before the ipo. He doesn't have more stuff, doesn't have more houses or anything that you could buy with money. response_text: Sacks makes the mechanics plain: Elon's balance sheet didn't change — the public simply repriced shares he already held, which he's locked up and not selling.

cited_text: I think it was Joseph Schumpeter who explained this, that basically you've got this intelligentsia in a society like ours where they don't make things, they don't make the machines that make things, they just make words and, I guess, ideas. And they become resentful of the fact that there are all these people who are able to create not just stuff, but the machines that make the stuff. And that in our society, that is the thing that creates the greatest reward financially.
response_text: Sacks credits Schumpeter: the intellectual class produces hot air, not machines, and becomes resentful of those who create actual value — because the market rewards the latter far more.

cited_text: And they would have placed that bet 10 years ago, when the company was worth 100 billion or 10 billion or 500 billion, but they weren't allowed to because we have a corrupt system in our government that says rich people, the top 4 or 5% of this country are smart enough to buy private company stocks, and the 95% are too stupid to. And it's antiquated and it has to change. And you should be able to buy whatever you want. If you want to buy crypto, you can. If you want to buy a private company, you can.
response_text: Calacanis on accredited investor rules: the top 4-5% can buy private company stock, the other 95% legally can't — even though they were watching SpaceX launch rockets for a decade and would have backed it.
